content:
3 min read
Just now

--

Building a safer, more predictable alternative to JS — with lightweight arrays, WASM support, and a fast Go runtime.
I’ve always been fascinated by JavaScript — its flexibility, ubiquity, and how it powers everything from browsers to servers. But many developers have run into its quirks: accidental mutations, prototype pollution, and behaviors that trip up beginners and pros alike. It powers the frontend, and is not the best for full stack applications especially when it comes to the DOM.
That’s why I created ArachnoScript (AS) — a high-level, multi-paradigm, dynamically typed language that feels instantly familiar to JS developers while introducing deliberate improvements for safety, performance, and real-world use cases.
Why ArachnoScript?
ArachnoScript isn’t here to replace JavaScript. It’s designed to complement it, especially for backend development, scripting, desktop/mobile GUIs (via web tech), and full-stack experiments. Key differences that make it shine:

• Arrays are lightweight (not objects)— Closer to C/Go behavior for better performance and predictability.

• Explicit runtime types (class, instance, macro, raw) and strong immutability options.

• Modern syntax features: Classes with inheritance/private/public fields/getters/setters, async/await, match expressions, array/object destructuring, and more.

• Backend-first focus with excellent server-side capabilities.
The result is a language that feels like JS but behaves more reliably.
The ArachnoScript Runtime Environment (ARE)
The heart of ArachnoScript is ARE — a robust, cross-platform runtime written in Go for speed and portability.
Highlights of the v0.2 series (with v0.2.5 as a major leap):

• Complete parser rewrite for a lighter, faster AST.

• WebAssembly (WASM) support + WAT-to-WASM compilation.

• Stable Foreign Function Interface (FFI) for seamless integration with other languages/libraries.

• Expanded bitwise operations, file caching for performance, rebuilt runtime architecture.

• Embedded standard library for faster startups.

• Improved CLI, REPL, macros, modules (HTTP, regexp, paths, encoding, date, etc.).

• Cross-platform installers for Windows, macOS, and Linux.
Keyword updates in recent versions make it even cleaner (e.g., `let`/`const` semantics).
Real-World Use Cases

• Backend & Servers: HTTP modules and strong concurrency and parallelism support.

• Scripting & Automation: Quick, safe scripts with excellent standard library.

• Web & Full-Stack: ASX (JSX-inspired) for UI components + Verdex for web/server APIs. Pair with JS on the frontend.
Getting Started (Super Easy)

• Install via the one-liner (macOS/Linux) or download the Windows installer from releases.

• Run `are` for the REPL or `are run yourfile.as`.

• Explore examples and full docs on the project sites.
Resources:

• GitHub: ECMA-King87/ArachnoScript-Runtime-Engine-ARE-

• Documentation & Intro: Buzzly Introduction

• Latest Updates & Community: Buzzly, Discord, and GitHub releases.

• License: Free for learning and personal use (check repo for details).
What’s Next for ArachnoScript?

• Deeper performance optimizations (potential VM/JIT).

• Expanded ecosystem, community contributions, and tooling.

• Richer standard library and web/desktop integrations.
ArachnoScript is still young but evolving fast — v0.2.5 brings it to a new level of maturity. If you love tinkering with languages, building backends, or want a fresh take on JS-style development, give it a try!
What do you think? Would you use ArachnoScript for backend work, scripting, or full-stack projects? Drop your thoughts, feature requests, or what you’ve built in the comments. I read every one and the project benefits hugely from community feedback.
Thanks for reading! Follow for more updates on ArachnoScript and the language design experiments.
